epigraph.text:
Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, as 
working for the Lord, not for human masters.

content.text:
In physics, *work* is what happens when a force moves an object through 
a distance. If you push a crate across a floor with a constant force of 
50 newtons over 10 meters, you do $W = 50 \times 10 = 500$ joules of work. 
Simple enough.

But what if the force *varies*? A spring resists more as you stretch 
it further. The weight of a chain changes as you lift it. The pressure 
on water being pumped out of a tank depends on the depth. In all these 
situations, the force is a function of position, and we need calculus to 
compute the total work.

Once again, the pattern is the same: **Slice, Approximate, Sum, 
Integrate.**
Work Done by a Variable Force

Suppose a variable force $F(x)$ acts on an object as it moves along the 
$x$-axis from $x = a$ to $x = b$.

**Step 1: Slice.** Divide $[a,b]$ into $n$ small subintervals of 
width $\Delta x$.

**Step 2: Approximate.** On each subinterval, the force is 
approximately constant at $F(x_k^*)$, so the work done over this small 
displacement is approximately
\[
 \Delta W_k \approx F(x_k^*)\,\Delta x.
\]

content.title: Work Done by a Variable Force
content.content:
If $F$ is a continuous force function on $[a,b]$, the 
**work** done in moving an object from $x = a$ to $x = b$ 
is
\[
 W = \defint{a}{b} F(x)\dx.
\]

content.text:
The SI unit of work is the **joule** (J): $1\,\text{J} = 1\,\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$. 
In the British system, work is measured in foot-pounds (ft-lb).
Hooke's Law: Work on a Spring

**Hooke's Law** states that the force required to 
stretch (or compress) a spring $x$ units beyond its natural length is 
proportional to $x$:
\[
 F(x) = kx,
\]
where $k > 0$ is the **spring constant** (in N/m or lb/ft). A stiff 
spring has a large $k$; a soft spring has a small $k$.

content.title: Work to stretch a spring
content.problem:
A spring has a natural length of 20 cm. A force of 25 N is required to 
hold it stretched to 30 cm. How much work is done in stretching it from 
20 cm to 35 cm?
content.solution:
First, find the spring constant. At $x = 10\,\text{cm} = 0.1\,\text{m}$ 
beyond natural length, $F = 25\,\text{N}$:
\[
 25 = k(0.1) \implies k = 250\;\text{N/m}.
\]
The work to stretch from $x = 0$ to $x = 0.15\,\text{m}$ (natural to 35 cm):
\[
 W = \defint{0}{0.15} 250x\dx = 250\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^{0.15} 
 = 250\cdot\frac{0.0225}{2} = 2.8125\;\text{J}.
\]

content.title: Work between two stretched positions
content.problem:
Using the same spring ($k = 250$ N/m), find the work required to stretch it 
from 30 cm to 40 cm.
content.solution:
From 30 cm to 40 cm is from $x = 0.1$ m to $x = 0.2$ m beyond natural 
length:
$$
 W &= \defint{0.1}{0.2} 250x\dx = 250\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{0.1}^{0.2} \\
 &= 125(0.04 - 0.01) = 125(0.03) = 3.75\;\text{J}.
$$
Notice this is *more* work than stretching from 20 to 30 cm 
($W = 1.25$ J), even though both intervals are 10 cm. The spring resists 
more when it is already stretched.

content.title: Finding the spring constant from work
content.problem:
It takes 4 J of work to stretch a spring from its natural length to 0.1 m 
beyond natural length. Find the spring constant.

content.text:
One of the most important (and most challenging) applications of the work 
integral is computing the work required to pump fluid out of a tank. The 
force is not constant because different layers of fluid must be lifted 
different distances.

content.title: Work to Pump Fluid
content.content:
1. Set up a coordinate axis (usually vertical, with $y$ increasing 
 upward).

2. **Slice** the fluid into thin horizontal layers of 
 thickness $\Delta y$.

3. For each layer at height $y$:
 itemize

4. Find the **volume** of the layer: $\Delta V = A(y)\,\Delta y$, 
 where $A(y)$ is the cross-sectional area.

5. Find the **weight** (force): 
 $\Delta F = \rho\,g\,A(y)\,\Delta y$ (metric) or 
 $\Delta F = \rho\,A(y)\,\Delta y$ (if $\rho$ already includes $g$).

6. Find the **distance** the layer must be lifted to reach 
 the top (or the spout).
 itemize

7. $\displaystyle W = \defint{a}{b} (\text{weight of layer}) 
 \times (\text{lift distance})\dy$.

content.text: We use the following physical constants:

items:
Weight density of water: $\rho g \approx 9800\;\text{N/m}^3$ (metric) 
 or $62.4\;\text{lb/ft}^3$ (British, already includes gravity).

content.title: Pumping water from a cylindrical tank
content.problem:
A cylindrical tank of radius 3 ft and height 8 ft is full of water. 
Find the work required to pump all the water over the top of the tank. 
(Use $\rho = 62.4$ lb/ft$^3$.)
content.solution:
Place the origin at the bottom of the tank with $y$ pointing up.

**Slice.** A thin horizontal layer at height $y$ has thickness 
$\Delta y$. Since the tank is a cylinder of radius 3:
\[
 A(y) = \pi(3)^2 = 9\pi \quad\text{(constant, independent of $y$)}.
\]
**Weight** of the layer: $62.4 \cdot 9\pi\,\Delta y$.

**Lift distance:** The layer at height $y$ must be lifted to $y = 8$, 
a distance of $(8 - y)$.

$$
 W &= \defint{0}{8} 62.4\cdot 9\pi\cdot(8-y)\dy 
 = 561.6\pi\defint{0}{8}(8-y)\dy \\
 &= 561.6\pi\left[8y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right]_0^8 
 = 561.6\pi(64 - 32) = 561.6\pi\cdot 32 \\
 &= 17{,}971.2\pi \approx 56{,}450\;\text{ft-lb}.
$$

content.title: Pumping water from a conical tank
content.problem:
A conical tank (vertex at the bottom) has a radius of 4 m at the top and a 
height of 10 m. It is filled with water to a depth of 8 m. Find the work 
required to pump all the water to the top of the tank. 
(Use $\rho g = 9800$ N/m$^3$.)
content.solution:
Place the origin at the vertex (bottom of the cone), with $y$ pointing up.

At height $y$, the radius of the cross-section is determined by similar 
triangles: $\dfrac{r}{y} = \dfrac{4}{10}$, so $r = \dfrac{2y}{5}$.

Cross-sectional area: $A(y) = \pi r^2 = \pi\left(\dfrac{2y}{5}\right)^2 
= \dfrac{4\pi y^2}{25}$.

The layer at height $y$ must be lifted to $y = 10$, a distance of $(10-y)$.

$$
 W &= \defint{0}{8} 9800\cdot\frac{4\pi y^2}{25}\cdot(10-y)\dy 
 = \frac{39{,}200\pi}{25}\defint{0}{8}y^2(10-y)\dy \\
 &= 1568\pi\defint{0}{8}(10y^2 - y^3)\dy \\
 &= 1568\pi\left[\frac{10y^3}{3} - \frac{y^4}{4}\right]_0^8 \\
 &= 1568\pi\left(\frac{5120}{3} - 1024\right) 
 = 1568\pi\cdot\frac{2048}{3} \\
 &= \frac{3{,}211{,}264\pi}{3} \approx 3{,}361{,}000\;\text{J} 
 \approx 3361\;\text{kJ}.
$$

content.title: Pumping from a hemispherical tank
content.problem:
A hemispherical tank of radius 5 ft (flat side up) is full of water. The 
water must be pumped to a pipe 2 ft above the top of the tank. Find the 
work. (Use $\rho = 62.4$ lb/ft$^3$.)
content.solution:
Place the origin at the center of the flat top, with $y$ pointing 
*downward* into the hemisphere. Then the hemisphere extends from 
$y = 0$ to $y = 5$. At depth $y$, the radius of the cross-section 
satisfies $r^2 + y^2 = 25$, so $r^2 = 25 - y^2$ and 
$A(y) = \pi(25 - y^2)$.

A layer at depth $y$ below the top must be lifted $(y + 2)$ feet 
(to reach 2 ft above the top).

$$
 W &= \defint{0}{5} 62.4\pi(25 - y^2)(y+2)\dy \\
 &= 62.4\pi\defint{0}{5}(25y + 50 - y^3 - 2y^2)\dy \\
 &= 62.4\pi\left[\frac{25y^2}{2} + 50y - \frac{y^4}{4} 
 - \frac{2y^3}{3}\right]_0^5 \\
 &= 62.4\pi\left(\frac{625}{2} + 250 - \frac{625}{4} - \frac{250}{3}\right) \\
 &= 62.4\pi\left(\frac{1250 + 1000 - 625}{4} - \frac{250}{3}\right) \\
 &= 62.4\pi\left(\frac{1625}{4} - \frac{250}{3}\right) 
 = 62.4\pi\cdot\frac{3875}{12} \\
 &= 62.4\pi\cdot\frac{3875}{12} = \frac{241{,}800\pi}{12} 
 = 20{,}150\pi \approx 63{,}310\;\text{ft-lb}.
$$

content.text:
When you lift a heavy cable (or chain), the amount of cable still hanging 
decreases as you pull. Therefore, the force (weight of the remaining cable) 
changes with position.

content.title: Lifting a hanging cable
content.problem:
A cable weighing 2 lb/ft hangs from the top of a 100-ft building. How much 
work is required to pull the entire cable to the top?
content.solution:
Let $x$ denote the distance (in feet) the cable has already been pulled up. 
When $x$ feet have been pulled, the remaining cable has length $(100 - x)$ ft 
and weighs $2(100 - x)$ lb.

**Slice:** Pull an additional $\Delta x$ feet. The force is 
approximately $F(x) = 2(100-x)$ during this small displacement.
$$
 W &= \defint{0}{100} 2(100-x)\dx 
 = 2\left[100x - \frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^{100} \\
 &= 2\left(10{,}000 - 5{,}000\right) = 10{,}000\;\text{ft-lb}.
$$

content.title: Cable with a weight attached
content.problem:
A 50-ft cable weighing 3 lb/ft is attached to a 200-lb bucket of coal at 
the bottom of a mine shaft. Find the work to lift the bucket and cable to 
the top.
content.solution:
The work has two parts:

**Part 1: Lifting the bucket.** Constant force of 200 lb over 50 ft:
\[
 W_1 = 200 \times 50 = 10{,}000\;\text{ft-lb}.
\]

**Part 2: Lifting the cable.** When $x$ ft have been pulled, the 
remaining cable weighs $3(50-x)$ lb:
\[
 W_2 = \defint{0}{50}3(50-x)\dx = 3\left[50x - \frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^{50} 
 = 3(2500 - 1250) = 3750\;\text{ft-lb}.
\]

Total: $W = 10{,}000 + 3{,}750 = 13{,}750\;\text{ft-lb}$.

content.title: Pulling half the cable
content.problem:
Using the same cable as Example [ex:cable-basic] (2 lb/ft, 100 ft), 
how much work is needed to pull only the *bottom half* (the first 
50 ft) to the top?
content.solution:
After pulling $x$ feet, the remaining cable weighs $2(100-x)$ lb. We 
pull from $x = 0$ to $x = 50$:
\[
 W = \defint{0}{50}2(100-x)\dx = 2\left[100x - \frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^{50} 
 = 2(5000 - 1250) = 7500\;\text{ft-lb}.
\]
This is $\frac{3}{4}$ of the total work $10{,}000$ ft-lb. It makes sense: 
the first half of the lift is harder because more cable is still hanging.

content.text:
The work–energy theorem from physics states that the net work done on an 
object equals the change in its kinetic energy:
\[
 W = \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}mv_f^2 - \frac{1}{2}mv_i^2.
\]
This is a direct consequence of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus applied 
to Newton's second law $F = ma$. Here we see once again how the integral 
connects a *rate* (force) to a *total* (work), just as it connects 
velocity to displacement.

content.title: Robert Hooke (1635--1703)
content.content:
Robert Hooke, a polymath and contemporary of Newton, published his law of 
elasticity in 1678 as a Latin anagram: "ceiiinosssttuv," which decoded to 
"ut tensio, sic vis" ("as the extension, so the force"). Hooke was a 
brilliant experimentalist who contributed to microscopy, astronomy, and 
architecture. His rivalry with Newton is legendary; Newton allegedly 
suppressed Hooke's contributions after Hooke's death. Yet Hooke's Law 
remains one of the most widely applied principles in physics and engineering.

marginNotes.text:
The Lord God took the man and put him in the 
Garden of Eden to work it and take care of it.

A cord of three strands is not quickly 
broken.

In your unfailing love you will lead the 
people you have redeemed. In your strength you will guide them to your 
holy dwelling.

marginNotes.author: James Prescott Joule (1818--1889)
marginNotes.text:
The grand agents of nature 
are, by the Creator's fiat, indestructible.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal (1623--1662)
marginNotes.text:
All the unhappiness of men arises 
from one single fact, that they cannot stay quietly in their own room.

marginNotes.author: Lord Kelvin (1824--1907)
marginNotes.text:
When you can measure what you are 
speaking about, and express it in numbers, you know something about it.
